  SUBSTRING('database', 1, 4),    -- 'data'  (1-indexed!)
  REPLACE('cat', 'c', 'b'),       -- 'bat'
  CONCAT('Hello', ', ', 'World'), -- 'Hello, World'
  first_name || ' ' || last_name  -- concatenation shorthand in Postgres
FROM employees LIMIT 1;

🔢 Numeric & Date Functions

SELECT
  ROUND(salary / 12.0, 2)         AS monthly,
  CEIL(3.1),                       -- 4
  FLOOR(3.9),                      -- 3
  ABS(-500),                       -- 500
  MOD(17, 5),                      -- 2 (remainder)
  NOW(),                           -- current timestamp
  CURRENT_DATE,                    -- today's date
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM hire_date)     AS hire_year,
  AGE(NOW(), hire_date)            AS tenure
FROM employees;

📏 DISTINCT — Remove Duplicates

-- How many unique departments are there?
SELECT DISTINCT department_id
FROM   employees;

-- Distinct combinations
SELECT DISTINCT department_id, job_title
FROM   employees;

📝 SQL Style Guide (What Pro Code Looks Like)

-- Bad: all on one line, inconsistent case
select first_name,salary from employees where salary>50000

-- Good: keywords UPPER, identifiers lower, one clause per line
SELECT first_name,
       salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  salary > 50000;

Your team will read your SQL more than they'll read your Python. Clean formatting is professionalism.

💡 Practice Pattern

-- Template to always start with:
SELECT <columns or *>
FROM   <table_name>
[WHERE  <condition>]
[ORDER BY <column>]
[LIMIT  <n>];

title: WHERE: Filter Rows Like a Pro
eli5_summary: WHERE is the gatekeeper: only rows that pass the condition get through. Master WHERE and you can answer almost any business question.
analogy: Think of WHERE as a bouncer at a club. You set the rules — 'only people over 21 with a valid ID who aren't on the no-fly list.' The bouncer checks each person. Only those who match ALL your rules get in.
content_html:
🎯 The WHERE Clause

SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  salary > 70000;

WHERE is evaluated before SELECT — the DB first filters rows, then picks columns. This means WHERE can reference table columns even if you don't SELECT them.

⚖️ Comparison Operators

WHERE salary = 75000       -- exactly equal
WHERE salary != 75000      -- not equal (also: <>)
WHERE salary < 75000       -- less than
WHERE salary <= 75000      -- less than or equal
WHERE salary > 75000       -- greater than
WHERE salary >= 75000      -- greater than or equal

📋 IN — Match a List

-- Instead of: dept = 'Sales' OR dept = 'Marketing' OR dept = 'HR'
WHERE department IN ('Sales', 'Marketing', 'HR')

-- NOT IN — exclude a list
WHERE department NOT IN ('Temp', 'Contractor')

-- IN with a subquery (advanced — you'll use this a lot)
WHERE employee_id IN (SELECT employee_id FROM managers)

↔️ BETWEEN — Range Check

-- Inclusive on both ends: >= 50000 AND <= 80000
WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 80000

-- Dates work too
WHERE hire_date BETWEEN '2020-01-01' AND '2022-12-31'

-- NOT BETWEEN
WHERE salary NOT BETWEEN 40000 AND 50000

🔍 LIKE — Pattern Matching

-- % matches zero or more characters
WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com'     -- ends with @gmail.com
WHERE first_name LIKE 'A%'         -- starts with A
WHERE last_name LIKE '%son%'       -- contains 'son'

-- _ matches exactly ONE character
WHERE code LIKE 'A_C'              -- matches AXC, ABC, A1C ...

-- ILIKE is case-insensitive (PostgreSQL only)
WHERE first_name ILIKE 'alice'     -- matches Alice, ALICE, alice

-- NOT LIKE
WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@temp.com'

❓ IS NULL / IS NOT NULL

-- Find rows where the value is missing
WHERE manager_id IS NULL           -- they're top-level managers

-- Find rows where it exists
WHERE manager_id IS NOT NULL

-- NEVER write: WHERE manager_id = NULL  (this always returns 0 rows!)
-- NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (unknown), not TRUE

This is one of the top interview trip-ups. Always use IS NULL, never = NULL.

🔗 AND / OR / NOT — Combining Conditions

-- AND: both must be true
WHERE salary > 70000
  AND department = 'Engineering'

-- OR: at least one must be true
WHERE department = 'Sales'
   OR department = 'Marketing'

-- NOT: flip the condition
WHERE NOT department = 'Intern'

-- PARENTHESES control order of evaluation (critical!)
WHERE (department = 'Sales' OR department = 'Marketing')
  AND salary > 60000
-- vs
WHERE department = 'Sales'
   OR (department = 'Marketing' AND salary > 60000)  -- completely different!

🧠 AND/OR Truth Table

ABA AND BA OR B
TRUETRUETRUETRUE
TRUEFALSEFALSETRUE
FALSETRUEFALSETRUE
FALSEFALSEFALSEFALSE

⚠️ NULL Propagates in Conditions

-- If salary IS NULL:
WHERE salary > 50000    -- NULL > 50000 = NULL (not true) → row excluded
WHERE salary = NULL     -- NULL = NULL = NULL (not true) → ALWAYS 0 rows!

-- Safe NULL handling with COALESCE
WHERE COALESCE(salary, 0) > 50000  -- treats NULL salary as 0

🎓 Real-World WHERE Patterns

-- Find active employees hired in the last year
WHERE status = 'active'
  AND hire_date >= NOW() - INTERVAL '1 year'

-- Find suspicious data: salary with no manager and not a director
WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  AND job_title NOT LIKE '%Director%'
  AND job_title NOT LIKE '%VP%'
  AND job_title NOT LIKE '%Chief%'

-- Find records updated more than 90 days ago (stale data check)
WHERE last_updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'

title: ORDER BY, LIMIT & DISTINCT: Shape Your Results
eli5_summary: After filtering rows, you often want them in a specific order, or just the top 10, or without repeats. ORDER BY, LIMIT, and DISTINCT give you that control.
analogy: You've got a shuffled deck of filtered cards. ORDER BY sorts them face-up. LIMIT says 'just give me the top 5.' DISTINCT removes duplicate cards from the deck before you even start.
content_html:
📊 ORDER BY — Sort Results

-- Sort by salary, highest first
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC;

-- Sort by salary, lowest first (default)
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary ASC;

-- Multi-column sort: primary sort, then tie-breaker
SELECT first_name, department, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY department ASC,
         salary DESC;    -- within same dept, highest salary first

🔢 Sort by Column Position (Shorthand)

-- Column 2 is salary
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY 2 DESC;         -- same as ORDER BY salary DESC

-- Useful in long queries, but less readable — use sparingly

❓ NULLs in ORDER BY

-- PostgreSQL: NULLs sort LAST by default for ASC, FIRST for DESC
SELECT name, manager_id
FROM   employees
ORDER BY manager_id ASC;   -- NULLs appear at bottom

-- Override: put NULLs first
ORDER BY manager_id ASC NULLS FIRST;

-- Override: put NULLs last (even in DESC)
ORDER BY manager_id DESC NULLS LAST;

NULL sorting behaviour differs by database! In Oracle, NULLs sort first for ASC. In SQL Server, NULLs sort first for ASC too. PostgreSQL is the opposite. Know your database.

✂️ LIMIT & OFFSET — Pagination

-- Top 10 highest-paid employees
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Page 2: skip the first 10, get next 10
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10;     -- OFFSET = how many rows to skip

-- Page N formula:  OFFSET = (page_number - 1) * page_size
-- Page 3, 10 per page: OFFSET = (3-1)*10 = 20

Always include ORDER BY with LIMIT! Without it, the DB returns rows in an undefined order — different every time.

🚫 DISTINCT — Remove Duplicate Rows

-- How many unique departments exist?
SELECT DISTINCT department
FROM   employees
ORDER BY department;

-- Distinct combinations (both columns must match for a row to be considered duplicate)
SELECT DISTINCT department, job_title
FROM   employees
ORDER BY department, job_title;

-- Count of unique values (very common)
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT department) AS unique_departments
FROM   employees;

⚠️ DISTINCT vs GROUP BY

-- These two are equivalent for simple deduplication:
SELECT DISTINCT department FROM employees;
SELECT department FROM employees GROUP BY department;

-- But GROUP BY lets you add aggregates (DISTINCT does not):
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount
FROM   employees
GROUP BY department;    -- DISTINCT cannot do this

🔝 TOP-N Queries — Real Interview Pattern

-- Top 3 highest salaries
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 3;

-- Second highest salary (classic interview question!)
SELECT MAX(salary)
FROM   employees
WHERE  salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

-- Better approach using OFFSET:
SELECT DISTINCT salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;       -- skip the highest, get the next one

📐 Full Query Execution Order

-- SQL clauses are written in this order:
SELECT ...
FROM ...
WHERE ...
GROUP BY ...
HAVING ...
ORDER BY ...
LIMIT ...

-- But the DATABASE executes them in this order:
-- 1. FROM   (load the table)
-- 2. WHERE  (filter rows)
-- 3. GROUP BY (group filtered rows)
-- 4. HAVING (filter groups)
-- 5. SELECT (compute output columns)
-- 6. DISTINCT (remove duplicates)
-- 7. ORDER BY (sort)
-- 8. LIMIT  (slice)

This is why you cannot use a SELECT alias in a WHERE clause — WHERE runs before SELECT!

title: Aggregates: COUNT, SUM, AVG, GROUP BY & HAVING
eli5_summary: Aggregates collapse many rows into summary numbers. GROUP BY splits rows into buckets first, then aggregates each bucket separately.
analogy: You have a box of Lego bricks. COUNT() counts them all. SUM() adds up the numbers on each brick. AVG() finds the average. GROUP BY sorts them by colour first — then you aggregate each colour pile separately.
content_html:
📊 The 5 Core Aggregate Functions

SELECT
  COUNT(*)                AS total_rows,       -- counts ALL rows
  COUNT(manager_id)       AS has_manager,      -- counts non-NULL values only
  COUNT(DISTINCT dept_id) AS unique_depts,     -- counts unique non-NULL values
  SUM(salary)             AS payroll_total,
  AVG(salary)             AS avg_salary,
  MIN(salary)             AS lowest_salary,
  MAX(salary)             AS highest_salary
FROM employees;

⚠️ COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column)

-- employees table has 100 rows, 10 have manager_id = NULL

SELECT COUNT(*)           -- 100  (counts all rows including NULLs)
SELECT COUNT(manager_id)  -- 90   (skips NULLs)
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT manager_id)  -- unique non-NULL manager_ids

-- AVG also ignores NULLs:
-- 5 employees: salaries are 50000, 60000, 70000, NULL, NULL
SELECT AVG(salary)  -- (50000+60000+70000) / 3 = 60000  (NOT divided by 5!)
-- If you want to treat NULL as 0:
SELECT AVG(COALESCE(salary, 0))  -- (50000+60000+70000+0+0) / 5 = 36000

🗂️ GROUP BY — Aggregate Per Category

-- Headcount and average salary per department
SELECT   department,
         COUNT(*)        AS headcount,
         AVG(salary)     AS avg_salary,
         MIN(salary)     AS min_salary,
         MAX(salary)     AS max_salary,
         SUM(salary)     AS dept_payroll
FROM     employees
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

The golden rule: every column in SELECT that is NOT inside an aggregate function MUST appear in GROUP BY.

-- WRONG — department is not in GROUP BY
SELECT department, job_title, COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;    -- ERROR: job_title must be in GROUP BY or aggregate

-- CORRECT
SELECT department, job_title, COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department, job_title;

🔎 HAVING — Filter Groups (Not Rows)

-- WHERE filters individual rows (before grouping)
-- HAVING filters groups (after aggregation)

-- Departments with more than 5 employees AND average salary over 70k
SELECT   department,
         COUNT(*)    AS headcount,
         AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM     employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 5
  AND    AVG(salary) > 70000
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

🚦 WHERE vs HAVING — When to Use Each

-- Use WHERE to filter rows before grouping:
SELECT   department, COUNT(*)
FROM     employees
WHERE    hire_date >= '2022-01-01'    -- only recent hires
GROUP BY department;

-- Use HAVING to filter based on the aggregate result:
SELECT   department, COUNT(*)
FROM     employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 10;              -- only large departments

-- Both together:
SELECT   department, COUNT(*) AS headcount, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM     employees
WHERE    status = 'active'           -- filter rows first
GROUP BY department
HAVING   COUNT(*) >= 3               -- then filter groups
ORDER BY avg_sal DESC;

📈 Real-World Aggregate Patterns

-- Monthly revenue totals
SELECT   DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
         COUNT(*)                         AS orders,
         SUM(amount)                      AS revenue,
         AVG(amount)                      AS avg_order_value
FROM     orders
WHERE    status = 'completed'
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
ORDER BY month;

-- Percentage of total (a very useful pattern)
SELECT
  department,
  COUNT(*) AS headcount,
  ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 1) AS pct_of_total
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

-- Find departments where avg salary gap (max-min) is very large
SELECT   department,
         MAX(salary) - MIN(salary) AS salary_range
FROM     employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING   MAX(salary) - MIN(salary) > 30000
ORDER BY salary_range DESC;

🧩 GROUP BY ROLLUP — Subtotals

-- Get subtotals AND grand total in one query
SELECT   department,
         job_title,
         SUM(salary) AS payroll
FROM     employees
GROUP BY ROLLUP (department, job_title)
ORDER BY department NULLS LAST, job_title NULLS LAST;
-- NULL in department = grand total row
-- NULL in job_title = department subtotal row

title: JOINs Deep Dive: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL & Self Joins
eli5_summary: JOINs combine rows from two tables by matching a condition. Different JOIN types decide what happens when one side has no match.
analogy: Two guest lists: Table A (employees) and Table B (departments). INNER JOIN gives you only guests on BOTH lists. LEFT JOIN gives you everyone from Table A whether or not they're on Table B. FULL JOIN gives you everyone from either list.
content_html:
🔗 INNER JOIN — Only Matched Rows

SELECT e.first_name,
       e.salary,
       d.name AS department_name
FROM   employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;
-- Returns only rows where department_id exists in BOTH tables
-- Employees with no department → excluded
-- Departments with no employees → excluded

⬅️ LEFT JOIN — All From Left, Matched From Right

SELECT e.first_name,
       d.name AS department_name
FROM   employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;
-- ALL employees returned
-- Employees with no department get NULL for department_name
-- Departments with no employees → not shown (they're on the RIGHT)

LEFT JOIN is the most common JOIN type in data work — use it when you want to preserve all records from your primary table and optionally enrich with data from another.

➡️ RIGHT JOIN — All From Right, Matched From Left

-- Rarely used — just flip the tables and use LEFT JOIN instead
-- These are equivalent:
SELECT * FROM employees e RIGHT JOIN departments d ON ...
SELECT * FROM departments d LEFT JOIN employees e ON ...

↔️ FULL OUTER JOIN — All Rows From Both

SELECT e.first_name,
       d.name AS department_name
FROM   employees e
FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;
-- ALL employees (even those with no dept) AND
-- ALL departments (even those with no employees)
-- Unmatched rows get NULL on the other side

Use FULL JOIN to find rows that exist in one table but not the other — perfect for data quality checks.

❌ Finding Non-Matches (Anti-JOIN Pattern)

-- Employees with no department assigned:
SELECT e.first_name
FROM   employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE  d.department_id IS NULL;    -- the magic: IS NULL on the RIGHT table PK

-- Departments with no employees:
SELECT d.name
FROM   departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
WHERE  e.employee_id IS NULL;

This anti-JOIN pattern is used constantly in data quality work to find orphaned records.

💥 Row Explosion — The Biggest JOIN Trap

-- employees: 3 rows
-- departments: 2 rows
-- What if department_id is NOT unique in departments? (bad data)

-- Departments table with duplicates:
dept_id | name
10      | Engineering
10      | Engineering   ← duplicate!

-- Your JOIN result:
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;
-- Alice (dept 10) now matches TWICE → 2 rows for Alice!
-- Bob   (dept 10) now matches TWICE → 2 rows for Bob!
-- Result has MORE rows than employees table

Always verify: Is the JOIN key unique on the side you're joining to? If not, you'll get row explosions and wrong aggregates.

✅ Checking for Row Explosion

-- Before JOINing, check if the key is unique in the lookup table:
SELECT department_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM   departments
GROUP BY department_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;    -- any results here = duplicates = explosion risk

-- After JOINing, verify row count:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees;           -- 100 rows
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees
  JOIN departments USING (department_id); -- should still be ~100 rows

🔄 Self Join — A Table Joining Itself

-- employees table has manager_id (FK → employee_id in same table)
-- Find each employee with their manager's name:
SELECT e.first_name   AS employee,
       m.first_name   AS manager
FROM   employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.employee_id;
-- e = the employee
-- m = the same table, treated as "managers"
-- LEFT JOIN because top-level managers have no manager (NULL)

🎯 JOIN Type Decision Guide

ScenarioJOIN Type
Want only rows where both sides matchINNER JOIN
Keep all from left, optionally enrich from rightLEFT JOIN
Find records in left table with NO match in rightLEFT JOIN + WHERE right.pk IS NULL
Find all rows from both tables regardless of matchFULL OUTER JOIN
Combine rows from same table on a related columnSelf JOIN (LEFT JOIN)

📝 Multi-Table JOIN

SELECT e.first_name,
       d.name    AS department,
       r.title   AS role,
       l.city    AS office_city
FROM   employees    e
JOIN   departments  d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
JOIN   roles        r ON e.role_id       = r.role_id
LEFT JOIN locations l ON d.location_id  = l.location_id;
-- Chain as many JOINs as you need
-- Each JOIN adds one table — new ON condition matches the new table to existing columns

title: CTEs & Subqueries: Write SQL That Reads Like English
eli5_summary: A CTE (Common Table Expression) lets you name a query and reuse it — like creating a temporary table just for this query. Subqueries are queries nested inside another query.
analogy: Writing a report. A subquery is an inline footnote you have to read mid-sentence. A CTE is a named appendix at the top — you read it first, then the main text references it by name. CTEs make complex queries readable.
content_html:
🔵 Subqueries — Query Inside a Query

-- Find employees who earn more than the company average
SELECT first_name, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
--               ↑ This inner query runs first, returns a single number

📦 Subquery in FROM (Derived Table)

-- Subquery as a temporary table
SELECT dept_summary.department, dept_summary.avg_salary
FROM (
  SELECT   department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
  FROM     employees
  GROUP BY department
) AS dept_summary
WHERE dept_summary.avg_salary > 70000;
-- The parenthesised query is called a "derived table" or "inline view"

🟢 CTEs — The Readable Alternative

-- Same query, rewritten with a CTE:
WITH dept_summary AS (
  SELECT   department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
  FROM     employees
  GROUP BY department
)
SELECT department, avg_salary
FROM   dept_summary
WHERE  avg_salary > 70000;

-- Start with: WITH cte_name AS ( ... )
-- Then reference cte_name in the main SELECT as if it were a table

🔗 Multiple CTEs

WITH
  -- Step 1: active employees only
  active_employees AS (
    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE status = 'active'
  ),
  -- Step 2: average salary per department (of active employees)
  dept_avgs AS (
    SELECT   department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
    FROM     active_employees
    GROUP BY department_id
  ),
  -- Step 3: employees who earn above their dept average
  above_avg AS (
    SELECT   ae.first_name, ae.salary, da.avg_salary, ae.department_id
    FROM     active_employees ae
    JOIN     dept_avgs da USING (department_id)
    WHERE    ae.salary > da.avg_salary
  )
-- Final SELECT uses the last CTE
SELECT first_name, salary, avg_salary,
       ROUND(salary - avg_salary, 2) AS above_avg_by
FROM   above_avg
ORDER BY above_avg_by DESC;

CTEs are executed top-to-bottom. Later CTEs can reference earlier ones. This makes even complex 200-line queries readable — each CTE is a named logical step.

🔄 Correlated Subqueries

-- A correlated subquery references the OUTER query's rows
-- It runs once per outer row — can be slow on large tables

-- Find employees who earn above their OWN department's average
SELECT first_name, department_id, salary
FROM   employees e1
WHERE  salary > (
  SELECT AVG(salary)
  FROM   employees e2
  WHERE  e2.department_id = e1.department_id  -- references outer row's dept
);
-- For each row of e1, the inner query computes avg for that row's department

Correlated subqueries are elegant but slow at scale. Rewrite with CTEs or window functions for performance.

✔️ EXISTS — Check If Related Rows Exist

-- Managers: employees who manage at least one person
SELECT first_name
FROM   employees e
WHERE  EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM   employees sub
  WHERE  sub.manager_id = e.employee_id
);
-- EXISTS is faster than IN for large tables — stops at first match

-- NOT EXISTS — employees with no direct reports
SELECT first_name
FROM   employees e
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM employees sub WHERE sub.manager_id = e.employee_id
);

🎯 When to Use CTE vs Subquery

SituationUse
Simple one-off filter or scalar valueSubquery
Complex multi-step logicCTEs
Reusing the same result multiple times in one queryCTEs
Recursion (org hierarchies, BOM)Recursive CTEs
Performance-critical path (PostgreSQL optimises independently)Subquery in FROM

🌀 Recursive CTEs (Bonus: Org Chart)

-- Walk an employee hierarchy top-down
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
  -- Anchor: top-level managers (no manager)
  SELECT employee_id, first_name, manager_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM   employees
  WHERE  manager_id IS NULL

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive: each employee's reports
  SELECT e.employee_id, e.first_name, e.manager_id, ot.depth + 1
  FROM   employees e
  JOIN   org_tree ot ON e.manager_id = ot.employee_id
)
SELECT depth, first_name
FROM   org_tree
ORDER BY depth, first_name;

title: Window Functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG & LEAD
eli5_summary: Window functions let you do calculations ACROSS rows while keeping each row. It's like GROUP BY but you don't collapse the rows — each row keeps its own identity plus a new computed column.
analogy: Imagine ranking students in class. A GROUP BY query would collapse all students into one summary row per class. A window function adds a 'rank' column to EACH student's row — you see the rank AND the student's name and score together.
content_html:
🪟 What Is a Window Function?

-- Regular aggregate collapses rows:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;
-- Returns 1 row per department, loses individual employee rows

-- Window function keeps all rows:
SELECT first_name,
       department,
       salary,
       AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg
FROM   employees;
-- Returns ALL employees, with their department average added as a column!

The OVER() clause is what makes it a window function. Everything inside OVER defines the "window" of rows to include in the calculation.

📐 OVER() Syntax

function_name()
  OVER (
    PARTITION BY column1, column2   -- "group by" for the window (optional)
    ORDER BY column3 DESC           -- sort within the window (optional)
    ROWS BETWEEN ...                -- frame clause (optional)
  )

🔢 ROW_NUMBER() — Unique Sequential Number

-- Rank employees by salary within each department (no ties)
SELECT first_name,
       department,
       salary,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
         PARTITION BY department
         ORDER BY salary DESC
       ) AS rank_in_dept
FROM   employees;

-- Classic use: deduplicate — keep only the highest-salary row per dept:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
  FROM employees
)
SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1;

🏆 RANK() vs DENSE_RANK()

-- salary: 90000, 85000, 85000, 70000
-- ROW_NUMBER:  1, 2, 3, 4   (always unique)
-- RANK:        1, 2, 2, 4   (tie = same rank, SKIPS next number)
-- DENSE_RANK:  1, 2, 2, 3   (tie = same rank, does NOT skip)

SELECT first_name, salary,
       RANK()       OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS row_num
FROM   employees;

Use DENSE_RANK() when "3rd place" should exist even if two people tie for 2nd. Use RANK() when you want to skip (like Olympic medals — two silver = no bronze).

📈 LAG() & LEAD() — Look at Previous/Next Rows

-- Compare each month's revenue to the previous month
SELECT month,
       revenue,
       LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY month)   AS prev_month,
       revenue - LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS month_over_month_diff,
       LEAD(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY month)  AS next_month
FROM   monthly_revenue;

-- LAG(column, n, default) — look n rows BACK, return default if no row exists
-- LEAD(column, n, default) — look n rows FORWARD

-- Example: detect consecutive salary increases
SELECT first_name, year, salary,
       LAG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY first_name ORDER BY year) AS prev_salary,
       salary > LAG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY first_name ORDER BY year) AS got_raise
FROM   salary_history;

📊 Running Totals & Moving Averages

-- Running total of revenue
SELECT order_date, amount,
       SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total
FROM   orders;

-- 7-day moving average
SELECT order_date, amount,
       AVG(amount) OVER (
         ORDER BY order_date
         ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS moving_avg_7d
FROM   orders;

-- Cumulative percentage of total
SELECT product, revenue,
       SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS cumulative,
       SUM(revenue) OVER () AS grand_total,
       ROUND(100.0 * SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC)
             / SUM(revenue) OVER (), 1) AS cumulative_pct
FROM   product_sales;

🎓 NTILE() — Split Into Buckets

-- Divide employees into 4 salary quartiles
SELECT first_name, salary,
       NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS salary_quartile
FROM   employees;
-- 1 = bottom quartile, 4 = top quartile

-- PERCENT_RANK — percentile rank (0 to 1)
SELECT first_name, salary,
       PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS percentile
FROM   employees;

🚀 JD-Focused Patterns

-- Find the top-earning employee per department (most common interview Q)
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
  FROM employees
)
SELECT department_id, first_name, salary FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1;

-- Month-over-month growth rate
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at) AS month,
         COUNT(*) AS signups
  FROM users GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT month, signups,
       LAG(signups) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_signups,
       ROUND(100.0 * (signups - LAG(signups) OVER (ORDER BY month))
             / NULLIF(LAG(signups) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0), 1) AS growth_pct
FROM monthly;

title: Data Quality: Duplicates, Orphans & Stale Data
eli5_summary: Bad data causes bad decisions. This lesson teaches you the SQL patterns to detect and report on duplicates, missing values, broken foreign key links, and data that hasn't been updated in too long.
analogy: You're a detective auditing a company's filing system. You check: Are there duplicate case files? Are there files referencing clients who no longer exist? Are there files that haven't been touched in years?
content_html:
🔍 Why Data Quality Matters

In data roles, you're often the last line of defence before bad data reaches a dashboard or model. The JD asked for "data quality checks" — here are the patterns you'll use weekly.

👯 Finding Duplicates

-- Method 1: GROUP BY + HAVING (most common)
SELECT email,
       COUNT(*) AS occurrences
FROM   users
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY occurrences DESC;

-- Method 2: Find the actual duplicate rows (with IDs)
WITH dupe_check AS (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY email          -- partition by the "unique" key
           ORDER BY created_at DESC    -- keep the newest one
         ) AS rn
  FROM users
)
SELECT * FROM dupe_check WHERE rn > 1;  -- rn=1 is the keeper, rn>1 are dupes

-- Method 3: How many unique vs total (quick overview)
SELECT
  COUNT(*)                AS total_rows,
  COUNT(DISTINCT email)   AS unique_emails,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT email) AS duplicate_count
FROM users;

🗑️ Removing Duplicates (Safely)

-- Step 1: always preview what you'll delete first
WITH to_delete AS (
  SELECT id,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY email ORDER BY created_at DESC
         ) AS rn
  FROM users
)
SELECT * FROM to_delete WHERE rn > 1;

-- Step 2: delete only after verifying the preview
DELETE FROM users
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT id FROM (
    SELECT id,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
    FROM users
  ) ranked
  WHERE rn > 1
);

🔗 Orphaned Foreign Keys

-- Find orders that reference a customer_id that doesn't exist
SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id
FROM   orders o
LEFT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE  c.customer_id IS NULL;

-- Find employees in departments that don't exist
SELECT e.employee_id, e.first_name, e.department_id
FROM   employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE  d.department_id IS NULL;

-- Count all orphaned records (dashboard metric)
SELECT
  'orders' AS table_name,
  COUNT(*) AS orphaned_count
FROM orders o LEFT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE c.customer_id IS NULL;

❓ Missing / NULL Value Audit

-- Check NULL coverage for every column at once
SELECT
  COUNT(*)                                              AS total_rows,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE first_name IS NULL)            AS null_first_name,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE email IS NULL)                 AS null_email,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE phone IS NULL)                 AS null_phone,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE department_id IS NULL)         AS null_dept,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE email IS NULL)
        / COUNT(*), 1)                                  AS pct_null_email
FROM employees;

-- FILTER is PostgreSQL syntax — cleaner than CASE WHEN
-- CASE WHEN equivalent (works in all SQL dialects):
SUM(CASE WHEN email IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_email_count

🕰️ Stale Data Detection

-- Records not updated in > 90 days
SELECT id, name, last_updated_at,
       NOW() - last_updated_at AS age
FROM   products
WHERE  last_updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'
ORDER BY last_updated_at ASC;

-- Count stale records by category
SELECT category,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days') AS stale_30d,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days') AS stale_90d,
       COUNT(*) AS total
FROM products
GROUP BY category;

-- Find records where end_date < start_date (impossible range)
SELECT * FROM subscriptions
WHERE end_date < start_date;

📊 Data Quality Dashboard Query

-- One query to rule them all: a quality report for one table
SELECT
  'employees'                                     AS table_name,
  COUNT(*)                                        AS total_rows,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE email IS NULL)           AS missing_email,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE department_id IS NULL)   AS missing_dept,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (
     SELECT email FROM employees
     GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
   ) d)                                           AS duplicate_emails,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days') AS stale_rows
FROM employees;

✅ Value Range & Format Checks

-- Impossible values
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary < 0;
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE hire_date > NOW();  -- hired in future?
SELECT * FROM orders    WHERE quantity <= 0;

-- Format checks (emails should have @)
SELECT email FROM users WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@%.%';

-- Unexpected category values
SELECT DISTINCT status FROM orders
WHERE status NOT IN ('pending', 'completed', 'cancelled', 'refunded');

-- Outlier detection (values more than 3 std deviations from mean)
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT AVG(salary) AS avg_sal, STDDEV(salary) AS std_sal FROM employees
)
SELECT e.first_name, e.salary
FROM employees e, stats
WHERE ABS(e.salary - stats.avg_sal) > 3 * stats.std_sal;

title: Views: Save & Reuse Your Queries
eli5_summary: A VIEW is a saved SQL query that looks and behaves like a table. You query it with SELECT just like a real table, but it runs your saved query behind the scenes every time.
analogy: A VIEW is like a saved search in your email. You don't copy all the emails — you save the search criteria. Every time you open it, it re-runs the search and shows current results.
content_html:
📋 Creating a View

-- Create a view once
CREATE VIEW active_employees AS
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, department, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  status = 'active';

-- Now query it like a table, forever
SELECT * FROM active_employees;
SELECT first_name, salary FROM active_employees WHERE salary > 70000;
SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM active_employees GROUP BY department;

The view doesn't store data — it stores the query. Every time you SELECT from it, the query runs fresh.

🔄 Updating & Dropping Views

-- Replace an existing view (safer than DROP + CREATE)
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW active_employees AS
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, department, salary, hire_date
FROM   employees
WHERE  status = 'active';

-- Drop a view
DROP VIEW active_employees;

-- Drop only if it exists (no error if missing)
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS active_employees;

🎯 When to Use a View

Use CaseWhy
Hide complex JOINs from analystsThey just SELECT from the view
Enforce row-level securityGive users access to view, not the base table
Standardise business logic"Active employees" defined once, everywhere consistent
Simplify reporting queriesBI tools (Tableau, Looker) connect directly to views

🔒 Views for Security

-- Base table has salaries, SSNs, sensitive data
-- Create a view that exposes only safe columns
CREATE VIEW employees_public AS
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, department, hire_date
FROM   employees;

-- Grant access to the VIEW, not the table
GRANT SELECT ON employees_public TO analyst_role;
-- Analysts can never see salary or SSN — the view is their only window

⚡ Materialized Views — Cache the Result

-- Regular VIEW: runs query every time (slow if query is complex)
-- Materialized VIEW: stores the result, refreshed on demand (fast reads)

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW dept_salary_summary AS
SELECT   department,
         COUNT(*)    AS headcount,
         AVG(salary) AS avg_salary,
         SUM(salary) AS payroll
FROM     employees
GROUP BY department;

-- Query it (reads stored result — very fast)
SELECT * FROM dept_salary_summary ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

-- Refresh when underlying data changes
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW dept_salary_summary;

-- Refresh without locking reads (PostgreSQL 9.4+)
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY dept_salary_summary;

Use materialized views for expensive aggregation queries that power dashboards — refresh them on a schedule (e.g., every hour via pg_cron).

📐 Views vs CTEs vs Materialized Views

FeatureCTEViewMaterialized View
Reusable across sessionsNo (query-level only)YesYes
Stores dataNoNoYes
Automatically up-to-dateYesYesNo (manual refresh)
Read performanceSame as querySame as queryVery fast
Best forWithin one complex queryShared reusable logicDashboard caching

🔍 Inspecting Existing Views

-- List all views in the current schema (PostgreSQL)
SELECT table_name, view_definition
FROM   information_schema.views
WHERE  table_schema = 'public'
ORDER BY table_name;

-- See what a view does
\d+ view_name          -- psql shortcut: shows definition + column details

-- Check if a view is updatable
SELECT is_updatable FROM information_schema.views
WHERE table_name = 'active_employees';

✏️ Updatable Views

-- Simple views (no JOIN, GROUP BY, DISTINCT, UNION) are often updatable
UPDATE active_employees
SET    salary = 80000
WHERE  employee_id = 1;
-- This updates the underlying employees table!

-- Complex views are read-only — use INSTEAD OF triggers or rewrite as INSERT/UPDATE
-- on the base table directly

title: Debugging SQL: Row Explosions, NULLs & Wrong Totals
eli5_summary: SQL bugs are silent — the query runs but returns wrong answers. This lesson teaches you a systematic debugging toolkit so you always know WHY your numbers are right.
analogy: You're a plumber. A water leak (wrong results) could be anywhere in the pipe. You don't guess — you isolate each section and test it. SQL debugging is the same: test each CTE/JOIN step individually.
content_html:
🔎 The Debugging Mindset

SQL errors fall into two categories:

  • Syntax errors — easy, the DB tells you immediately
  • Logical errors — silent, query runs, returns plausible-looking but wrong data

Logical errors are dangerous. You need a systematic approach.

💥 Row Explosion Debugging

-- Symptom: aggregate results are 2x, 3x, or 10x too large
-- Cause: JOINing to a table where the key is not unique

-- Step 1: Check row count BEFORE and AFTER join
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders;                           -- 1000 rows

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id;      -- 4500 rows!
-- Each order has ~4.5 items on average — expected explosion

-- Step 2: Check if the JOIN key is unique in the lookup table
SELECT order_id, COUNT(*) FROM order_items
GROUP BY order_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;                -- confirms multiple items

-- Step 3: Decide: do you want one row per item, or one row per order?
-- One row per order (aggregate the items side):
SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id,
       COUNT(oi.item_id)  AS item_count,
       SUM(oi.price)      AS total_amount
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id, o.customer_id;

❓ NULL Pitfalls

-- Pitfall 1: NULL in comparisons
WHERE manager_id = NULL    -- ALWAYS returns 0 rows
WHERE manager_id != NULL   -- ALWAYS returns 0 rows
WHERE manager_id IS NULL   -- CORRECT

-- Pitfall 2: NULL in NOT IN
-- employees with manager_id: NULL, 1, 2, 3
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE manager_id NOT IN (1, 2);
-- Returns 0 rows! Because NULL NOT IN (1, 2) = NULL (not TRUE)
-- Fix: exclude NULLs explicitly
WHERE manager_id NOT IN (1, 2) AND manager_id IS NOT NULL

-- Pitfall 3: NULL in aggregates (already covered but important)
-- COUNT(column) skips NULLs, COUNT(*) does not
-- AVG(column) divides by non-NULL count only

-- Pitfall 4: String comparison with trailing spaces
WHERE status = 'active'    -- might fail if stored as 'active   '
WHERE TRIM(status) = 'active'   -- safe

🔢 Wrong Totals Debugging

-- Symptom: your SUM doesn't match the expected total
-- Technique: sanity check with a known number

-- Step 1: Get the "truth" from the source table
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders;          -- 1,000,000

-- Step 2: Get your query's total
SELECT SUM(o.amount)
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id;  -- 1,200,000 ???

-- Step 3: Check for row explosion in the JOIN
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders;             -- 5000
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id; -- 6000 → explosion!

-- Root cause: some customer_ids appear multiple times in customers
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM customers
GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

🐛 The Incremental Build Strategy

-- Don't write the full 50-line query, then debug
-- Build incrementally — test each step

-- Step 1: Does the base table look right?
SELECT * FROM orders LIMIT 10;
SELECT COUNT(*), MIN(order_date), MAX(order_date) FROM orders;

-- Step 2: Add one JOIN, verify row count
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id;

-- Step 3: Add filters, verify
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed';

-- Step 4: Add aggregation
SELECT c.country, SUM(o.amount)
FROM orders o JOIN customers c ...
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY c.country;

-- Each step should produce the expected number of rows

🔬 Isolating CTEs for Debugging

-- Your complex query:
WITH step1 AS (...),
     step2 AS (...),
     step3 AS (...)
SELECT * FROM step3;

-- Debug each CTE by temporarily running just that part:
WITH step1 AS (
  SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
  FROM employees GROUP BY department_id
)
SELECT * FROM step1;   -- Run just this, verify it looks right

-- Then add step2:
WITH step1 AS (...),
     step2 AS (
       SELECT * FROM step1 WHERE avg_salary > 70000
     )
SELECT * FROM step2;  -- Verify again

📏 Proving Your Query Is Correct

-- Technique: cross-check with a simpler query
-- Complex query says sales = 1,000,000 for Q1

-- Sanity check:
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31'
  AND status = 'completed';  -- should match

-- Check edge cases: what happens with no rows?
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE 1=0;  -- returns NULL, not 0
-- Does your code handle NULL totals?

-- Check row-level examples:
-- Pick 3 specific order IDs, manually verify your query returns them correctly

🛠️ Common SQL Bugs Cheat Sheet

SymptomLikely CauseFix
Result has more rows than expectedJOIN key not uniqueAdd GROUP BY or use DISTINCT
SUM is too largeRow explosionAggregate before joining
WHERE = NULL returns nothingNULL comparisonUse IS NULL
NOT IN returns 0 rowsNULL in the listAdd IS NOT NULL filter
Wrong GROUP BY resultsColumn not in GROUP BYAdd all non-aggregate SELECTs to GROUP BY
HAVING filter not workingUsing WHERE on aggregateMove to HAVING

title: Python + PostgreSQL: Automate SQL with psycopg2
eli5_summary: Python can talk directly to PostgreSQL using the psycopg2 library. This lets you automate data quality checks, generate reports, and build data pipelines without leaving Python.
analogy: SQL is like talking to a waiter (the database). psycopg2 is your phone — it lets Python call the waiter, place an order, and receive the food (results) back in Python format for further cooking.
content_html:
🔌 Connecting to PostgreSQL

import psycopg2
import psycopg2.extras   # for dict cursor

# Connection string approach
conn = psycopg2.connect(
    host="localhost",
    port=5432,
    database="adhd_dashboard",
    user="adhd",
    password="adhd_secret"
)

# Context manager (auto-closes)
with psycopg2.connect(dsn="postgresql://adhd:adhd_secret@localhost:5432/adhd_dashboard") as conn:
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("SELECT version()")
        print(cur.fetchone())

🔍 Querying Data

import psycopg2
import psycopg2.extras

conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn="postgresql://...")
cur = conn.cursor(cursor_factory=psycopg2.extras.DictCursor)  # rows as dicts

# Execute a query
cur.execute("SELECT first_name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary > %s", (70000,))
#                                                                           ↑ tuple!
# NEVER use f-strings for SQL — SQL injection risk!
# ALWAYS use %s placeholders with a tuple of values

# Fetch results
rows = cur.fetchall()
for row in rows:
    print(row['first_name'], row['salary'])  # DictCursor: access by column name

# Fetch one row
cur.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees")
count = cur.fetchone()[0]
print(f"Total employees: {count}")

cur.close()
conn.close()

🔒 Parameterised Queries (SQL Injection Prevention)

# DANGEROUS — never do this:
department = input("Enter department: ")
cur.execute(f"SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = '{department}'")
# If user types: ' OR 1=1 -- they get ALL employees!

# SAFE — always use parameterised queries:
department = input("Enter department: ")
cur.execute("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = %s", (department,))
# psycopg2 escapes the value properly

with psycopg2.connect(dsn="...") as conn:
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        # Insert a row
        cur.execute("""
            INSERT INTO employees (first_name, last_name, salary, department)
            VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s)
            RETURNING employee_id
        """, ("Jane", "Doe", 75000, "Engineering"))
        new_id = cur.fetchone()[0]
        print(f"Created employee ID: {new_id}")

        # Update rows
        cur.execute("""
            UPDATE employees
            SET salary = salary * 1.1
            WHERE department = %s AND status = 'active'
        """, ("Engineering",))
        print(f"Rows updated: {cur.rowcount}")

    conn.commit()  # commit inside 'with conn' block
    # or: conn.rollback() to undo

🤖 Automated Data Quality Report

import psycopg2, psycopg2.extras
from datetime import datetime

def run_quality_check(conn, table):
    checks = {}
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        # Total rows
        cur.execute(f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table}")
        checks['total_rows'] = cur.fetchone()[0]

        # NULL check on key columns
        cur.execute(f"""
            SELECT
              COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE email IS NULL) AS null_email,
              COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE department_id IS NULL) AS null_dept
            FROM {table}
        """)
        row = cur.fetchone()
        checks['null_email'] = row[0]
        checks['null_dept'] = row[1]

        # Duplicate emails
        cur.execute(f"""
            SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (
              SELECT email FROM {table}
              GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
            ) d
        """)
        checks['duplicate_emails'] = cur.fetchone()[0]

    return checks

if __name__ == '__main__':
    conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn="postgresql://adhd:adhd_secret@localhost:5432/adhd_dashboard")
    report = run_quality_check(conn, 'employees')
    print(f"[{datetime.now():%Y-%m-%d %H:%M}] Quality Report — employees")
    for key, val in report.items():
        status = '✅' if val == 0 or key == 'total_rows' else '❌'
        print(f"  {status} {key}: {val}")
    conn.close()
